survivors.claim: core-fix-release promises "the live-defect fix ships as its own patch release ahead of the pack" with `--change-class neither`, but the graph encodes no seam (pack-dispatch is a parallel sibling also depending only on core-fail-closed-file-args; engine-surface is a root; nothing keeps pack landings off main before the release), and the seam was already breached before the plan was committed: main carries the core fix plus engine-surface, pack-dispatch, workspace-split, and fixture-licensing, so cutting the release as written mints a change-class-neither "patch release" that actually ships the reserved `pack` verb, the lib-surface split, and the ffmpeg pack skeleton — a mislabeled release under the change-class doctrine (a new subcommand is feature class per MEMORY.md) whose verify greens anyway, a hollow green at the release seam. The plan simultaneously presents the five already-landed nodes uniformly as pending with future verify lines, so the receipted graph starts false and the live next action (core-fix-release, which closes #23) is indistinguishable from unstarted work.
survivors.evidence: Plan core-fix-release: "The live-defect fix ships as its own patch release ahead of the pack, so #23 does not wait on the pack build: `host-lifecycle release host-lint --change-class neither`" with "depends: core-fail-closed-file-args" only, while pack-dispatch also "depends: core-fail-closed-file-args" (no anti-edge) and engine-surface is "depends: none (root)". host-lint#23 comment (2026-07-17T14:07Z; README committed 15:22 same day, commit 2a6a93eb): "The core fix landed on main as 28b1fd5, with a regression test ... The first tasks of the revised sequence on #22 are also on main now, through the workspace split and the pack skeleton." Live host-lint main (API-confirmed, HEAD e75c6c4d): 28b1fd59 "fail closed on an explicit file argument that cannot be scanned", d5e9efc9 "move the reporting surface into the library", e46880c1 "dispatch external packs through a reserved pack verb", 3faa4c0b "split the repo into a workspace and add the ffmpeg pack skeleton", 8c758a65 "record the fixture licensing policy" — all after release commit 241a870b (v0.14.2, 2026-07-08). MEMORY.md line 686: "The change-class for a new subcommand is `feature`". Verify line "`host-lifecycle software --check .` clean at the new pin; #23 closed" passes regardless of what the release contains.
survivors.remedy: Record the landed-on-main state of the five early nodes in the build-sequence preamble (done, with commit ids, awaiting receipts and release) and mark core-fix-release as the live next action. Re-specify the release point: either cut from a branch at 28b1fd59 keeping `--change-class neither` (fix-only patch release, tag off-main), or accept releasing main HEAD, re-class the task adds-flag/feature, and drop the "own patch release ahead of the pack" claim. Encode the seam explicitly — make core-fix-release a stated dependency of every further host-lint-repo landing, or record the branch-cut instruction — so the seam cannot be contaminated further.
survivors.verify_evidence: Re-read the full README (/mnt/c/Users/dconnolly/Development/agentic-host/plan/0072-ffmpeg-commit-rule-pack/README.md), fetched host-lint#23 with comments and timestamps, listed live host-lint main via gh api, and read MEMORY.md ~line 686. Every quoted fact checks out. (1) Graph text exact: core-fix-release says "The live-defect fix ships as its own patch release ahead of the pack ... `host-lifecycle release host-lint --change-class neither`" with "depends: core-fail-closed-file-args"; pack-dispatch also "depends: core-fail-closed-file-args" (stated dependencies override document order per the preamble, so no seam edge exists); engine-surface is "depends: none (root)". Its verify line — "`host-lifecycle software --check .` clean at the new pin; #23 closed" — checks pin coherence and issue closure only, blind to release content, so it greens on a contaminated release: the hollow-green characterization is fair. (2) Breach confirmed live: gh api shows main HEAD e75c6c4d with 28b1fd59 (fail-closed fix), d5e9efc9 (reporting surface into library), e46880c1 (reserved pack verb), 3faa4c0b (workspace split + ffmpeg pack skeleton), 8c758a65 (fixture licensing) all after release commit 241a870b "release v0.14.2" (2026-07-08). A release cut from main as written ships the pack verb and skeleton under `--change-class neither`. (3) Timeline confirmed: the #23 comment recording the landings ("The core fix landed on main as 28b1fd5 ... The first tasks of the revised sequence on #22 are also on main now, through the workspace split and the pack skeleton") is timestamped 2026-07-17T14:07:32Z; the README commit 2a6a93eb is 2026-07-17T15:22:53+01:00 = 14:22 UTC, 15 minutes later — the plan was committed after the breach and after the operator recorded it. (4) Pending-presentation confirmed: grep for landed/already/28b1fd/branch/on-main finds nothing; no task carries a done marker, commit id, or branch-cut instruction, and the preamble affirmatively claims "the release seams made explicit for this host", so an executor reading the milestone (which calls itself the record: "What lives here is the milestone: ... the receipted task graph") treats core-fix-release as a clean next action. (5) Doctrine confirmed twice: MEMORY.md quote is verbatim ("The change-class for a new subcommand is `feature` (minor: 0.20.2 -> 0.21.0)"), and the plan's own docs-and-release task uses `--change-class adds-flag` for exactly this pack surface, so `neither` on a release carrying the new `pack` verb is misclassified by the plan's own internal standard, not just by MEMORY.md. No evidence host-lifecycle validates class against diff content (MEMORY.md records it silently ignoring `--preview`, i.e. it trusts the operator). Severity blocker stands: the live next action, executed as written, mints an outward and sticky wrong result — a mislabeled producer tag plus a .host-software re-pin under dual-release-authority — while the verify line greens. Two precision notes that do not change the verdict: the README commit time is 15:22 local / 14:22 UTC (the finding's "15:22 same day" is local time; the ordering claim holds), and MEMORY.md's `feature` belongs to host-lifecycle's own class vocabulary while this plan's vocabulary is removes-flag|adds-flag|neither — the equivalent here is adds-flag, which the remedy already names. Remedy is proportionate: the two release-point options (branch cut at 28b1fd59 keeping neither, or release HEAD re-classed adds-flag and drop the patch-release/ahead-of-the-pack claim) are the only coherent resolutions, and recording the landed state plus an explicit seam edge or branch-cut instruction addresses the graph defect directly.

survivors.claim: corpus-calibration ("every mechanical rule runs over several thousand recent accepted upstream commits ... Tiers freeze here and nowhere earlier", depends: msg-lane, diff-lane) is unexecutable at its graph position and its freeze cannot bind: series-lane depends ON corpus-calibration and lands ~9 mechanical rules after the freeze; mail-lane and forge-lane (depends: msg-lane) land ~10 more with no calibration downstream at all; cosmetic-separation's flag-tier cosmetic-mix classifier is a parallel sibling not feeding calibration; and mail/forge rules are structurally uncalibratable over a commit corpus (MIME type, To/Cc addressing, DMARC records, PR titles are not commit properties). The scope guarantee "Corpus calibration over accepted upstream history before any severity tier freezes" is therefore structurally unachievable as sequenced; the gate test ("no flag-tier mechanical rule exceeds the agreed rate") passes vacuously for every post-freeze rule or must silently exempt them; the registry's measured-rate fields, checklist-reporter's "measured rates alongside each mechanical result", and RULES.md's "each rule with its citation, measured rate" are unfulfillable; and forge-lane's verify hard-codes flag tier for rules never tiered or measured — the exact defect (#23 tiers-assigned-by-intuition / uncalibrated blockers) the plan claims settled, with tiers assigned by argument in the addendum instead of measurement.
survivors.evidence: corpus-calibration: "every mechanical rule runs over several thousand recent accepted upstream commits ... Tiers freeze here and nowhere earlier. — depends: msg-lane, diff-lane"; series-lane: "depends: cosmetic-separation, corpus-calibration" while landing "alphabetical-order, maintainers-coverage, checkasm-obligation, gpl-gating, doc-updated, and shared-abi ... the backport-fix focus warn ... the fate-sample checks ... the security three-way routing classifier"; mail-lane lands "the per-message 1000 kB flag ... the DMARC pre-send warn" etc.; forge-lane's verify asserts "a bare-branch-name title flags ... a versioned title flags" while the addendum only says "New check: PR titles satisfy the subject grammar" (no tier). Settled architecture (a stated constraint on every task): "Severity tiers freeze only after calibration against accepted upstream history, and the registry records each mechanical rule's measured false-positive rate ... Intuition proposes; measurement disposes"; docs-and-release: "RULES.md (the corpus in human form, each rule with its citation, measured rate...)"; the #22 addendum assigns tiers by reasoning ("flag tier is justified by the silent-stall failure mode"). The uncalibrated-flag hazard is proven live by #23's include-exists-flags-generated-headers, a series-lane flag rule whose false-positive mode was caught only by review. PLAN.md's row repeats the "frozen only after calibration" claim.
survivors.remedy: Re-cut the graph: run corpus-calibration after series-lane (its rules are calibratable over commit history) or add a second calibration/re-freeze gate after series-lane, mail-lane, and forge-lane before checklist-reporter or at latest docs-and-release; add cosmetic-separation to calibration's depends; bind the gate test to a digest of the calibrated rule set so any rule added or changed after the report fails the gate instead of passing vacuously; name an honest calibration corpus for mail/forge rules (patchwork/mail-archive series, or reconstructed format-patch output from accepted series) or record their tiers as explicit uncalibratable/argument-derived registry states with the cited failure mode (e.g. the FAQ's silent moderation stall for the 1000 kB flag); downgrade forge-lane's verify tier assertions to provisional-until-measured; update the PLAN.md row.
survivors.verify_evidence: Read plan/0072 README in full, the #22 record (pgs9 addendum, consolidated revision, public-signals addendum), #23's body, and the digest's calibration/tier lines. Every load-bearing quote checks out. (1) Graph: corpus-calibration says verbatim "every mechanical rule runs over several thousand recent accepted upstream commits ... Tiers freeze here and nowhere earlier" with "depends: msg-lane, diff-lane"; series-lane ("depends: cosmetic-separation, corpus-calibration") lands ~9 mechanical rules after the freeze; mail-lane and forge-lane ("depends: msg-lane") are unordered siblings with no calibration downstream anywhere; cosmetic-separation is absent from calibration's depends and the README's ordering rule ("stated dependencies override") means its flag-tier cosmetic-mix classifier need not exist at calibration time. The freeze therefore cannot cover the majority of the mechanical corpus — the implementations it must run do not exist at its graph position. (2) Tier words are precise in this repo (flag=exit 1, warn=exit 3, defined in the README's own GitHub-usage doctrine and used consistently: "stale receipt warns (exit 3)"), so forge-lane's verify "a bare-branch-name title flags ... a versioned title flags" and mail-lane's "per-message 1000 kB flag" are genuine flag-tier assignments never measured; the #22 addendum assigns the latter by argument, verbatim "flag tier is justified by the silent-stall failure mode" — exactly #23 tiers-assigned-by-intuition ("The pack design assigns tiers by judgment"; remedy "run every mechanical rule ... before the tiers freeze"), which the README claims fully folded in. (3) Nothing elsewhere handles it: no re-calibration or second freeze task exists; docs-and-release's verify is "`host-lint --docs` clean; id-sync green; tag and assets present" with no rate check; open decision 5 is only the threshold value. Yet scope promises "Corpus calibration over accepted upstream history before any severity tier freezes", checklist-reporter promises "measured rates alongside each mechanical result", RULES.md "each rule with its citation, measured rate", and the PLAN.md row repeats "severity tiers frozen only after calibration over accepted upstream history" — unfulfillable for post-freeze rules, and the gate test ("no flag-tier mechanical rule exceeds the agreed rate on the calibration corpus") passes hollow for rules never run over the corpus, a plan/0052 no-hollow-green violation inside the measurement-discipline task itself. (4) The structural-uncalibratability claim is accurate as scoped: MIME type, To/Cc, sender-domain DMARC, and PR titles are mail/forge properties, not commit properties; the addendum's "sampled merged-PR titles" is an informal sample, not the mandated calibration. Remedy checked and proportionate: re-positioning or re-running calibration (which entails inverting the series-lane→corpus-calibration edge), digest-binding the gate, honest mail/forge corpora (mail-archive/patchwork, reconstructed format-patch output) or explicit argument-derived registry states, provisional forge verify tiers, PLAN.md row update. Sole nuance: "unexecutable at its graph position" mildly overstates — the task can run over the msg+diff rules that exist; it is the "every mechanical rule" contract and the bindingness of the freeze that are unsatisfiable, which the finding itself states ("its freeze cannot bind"). Wording nuance only; substance, severity (blocker: executed as written the plan yields a release whose headline measurement guarantee is false for most mechanical rules, with unmeasured flag-tier rules gating real submissions behind a vacuous green gate), and remedy all stand.

survivors.claim: hook-installer's verify includes "the skew case refuses", but the runtime version handshake ("the pack refuses to run on a major or minor mismatch with the core, exit 2 with both versions named", via the core's HOST_LINT_VERSION export) is delivered by pack-dispatch, which is not in hook-installer's transitive dependency closure (msg-lane/diff-lane/project-pack-config all root through engine-surface, not core-fail-closed-file-args), and pack-dispatch has no dependent before docs-and-release so it can legitimately schedule after hook-installer — the skew verify then either cannot run or silently tests only the install-time stamp comparison while the installed hook, invoking the pack binary directly, bypasses the handshake entirely (the exact fail-open #23's version-handshake-fails-open condemned).
survivors.evidence: "### hook-installer ... depends: msg-lane, diff-lane, project-pack-config ... verify by: ... the skew case refuses"; the handshake is specified under pack-dispatch ("strict version handshake with refusal on skew") and in the settled architecture ("exit 2 with both versions named"); no other task lists pack-dispatch as a dependency until docs-and-release's "depends: everything above". Opening design: "The core exports `HOST_LINT_VERSION` into the child's environment" — the pack-side refusal needs the dispatch path.
survivors.remedy: Add pack-dispatch to hook-installer's depends and state that the installed hooks invoke the pack through `host-lint pack ffmpeg` (not the pack binary directly), so the skew verify exercises the runtime refusal, not only the installer's stamp check.
survivors.verify_evidence: Dependency closure verified from the README depends lines: hook-installer "depends: msg-lane, diff-lane, project-pack-config"; msg-lane and project-pack-config root through rule-registry → workspace-split → engine-surface, diff-lane adds fixture-licensing (also a root) — pack-dispatch ("depends: core-fail-closed-file-args") is in neither closure, and a scan of every task confirms no task lists pack-dispatch as a dependency before docs-and-release's "depends: everything above". The README's own scheduling rule ("Tasks with no stated dependency follow the previous task; stated dependencies override") means hook-installer's stated depends override document order, so pack-dispatch legitimately schedules after hook-installer, and under plan/0042 doctrine the depends lines are the normative graph. The mechanism claim checks out against the design record: issue #22's opening post defines the hook path as routed through core dispatch ("`host-lint ffmpeg msg [--stdin | <file>]`: one commit message (the commit-msg hook path)") and the handshake as dispatch-borne ("The core exports `HOST_LINT_VERSION` into the child's environment; a pack may warn on a version skew it cannot tolerate"), which the consolidated revision hardened to "the pack refuses to run on a major or minor mismatch with the core" (README settled architecture: "exit 2 with both versions named") after #23's version-handshake-fails-open condemned the warn as repeating a recorded pgs-release hazard ("hook installs copy binaries, upgrades do not refresh the copies, and PATH installs drift independently"). One nuance that narrows but does not defeat the finding: the consolidated revision deliberately splits the version-handshake-fails-open remedy across both tasks — pack-dispatch owns the pack-side runtime refusal (verified there by stub-pack "skew refusal" tests), hook-installer owns the installer half ("The installer stamps core and pack versions together and refuses a skewed pair") — so hook-installer's "the skew case refuses" can technically run without pack-dispatch as an installer-level stamp check. But that is exactly the finding's second disjunct: run that way it verifies only install-time stamping, which by #23's own analysis does not protect against post-install drift, and a hook built before dispatch exists must invoke the pack binary directly, where HOST_LINT_VERSION is absent and the refusal-on-mismatch rule is silent (absence is not a "mismatch"), reintroducing the fail-open. The defect is actually wider than the skew bullet — hook-installer's "a staged tab is blocked" verify also exercises the pack diff lane through the installed hook, which per the design record routes through dispatch — strengthening the finding. The remedy (add the pack-dispatch edge; state the hooks invoke via `host-lint pack ffmpeg`) matches the opening design's hook path and the settled architecture, and is proportionate. Severity major is right: a missing edge in a receipted graph whose legitimate schedules either block the task mid-execution or ship the exact fail-open the review condemned is a hole in a promised guarantee, not a contained nit.

survivors.claim: spec-obligations (depends: series-lane, checklist-reporter) can be verified and receipted while mail-lane and forge-lane — parallel branches off msg-lane with no ordering against it — are still unbuilt; under parallel dispatch those lanes then land byte-level parse code (the MAINTAINERS parser with three CC syntaxes, MIME/size checks, the PR-title grammar) that the frozen `ffmpeg-pack.allium`, `.obligations` manifest, Kani harnesses, and proptest coverage never modelled, forcing regeneration and obligation re-disposition — and no later verify re-runs the spec gate (docs-and-release verifies only "`host-lint --docs` clean; id-sync green; tag and assets present; #22 closed"), so the spec-discipline receipt goes stale silently.
survivors.evidence: "### spec-obligations ... `ffmpeg-pack.allium` modelling the verdict lifecycle and rule tiers ... Kani harnesses for the byte-level predicates, proptest over open input spaces. — depends: series-lane, checklist-reporter" while "### mail-lane ... depends: msg-lane" and "### forge-lane ... depends: msg-lane" carry "the MAINTAINERS parser passes the live-syntax fixture set (three CC forms, the non-CC parenthesized address, the section-scoped duplicate globs)" — exactly the open-input-space parser the spec task's proptest/Kani bar targets. survivors.remedy: Add mail-lane and forge-lane to spec-obligations' depends (one-line graph fix), so the spec models the full shipped lane set before its receipt is cut.
survivors.verify_evidence: Read the full README (/mnt/c/Users/dconnolly/Development/agentic-host/plan/0072-ffmpeg-commit-rule-pack/README.md), the #22 opening post, its consolidated revision and public-signals addendum, and #23. Every load-bearing claim checks out. (1) Graph: spec-obligations reads "- depends: series-lane, checklist-reporter" (line 418); mail-lane and forge-lane each read "- depends: msg-lane" (lines 340, 358); a scan of all 22 depends lines confirms nothing consumes mail-lane or forge-lane except docs-and-release's "- depends: everything above", and spec-obligations' transitive closure (series-lane, checklist-reporter → msg/diff/cosmetic/calibration/build-receipts) never reaches either lane. The README's rule "Tasks with no stated dependency follow the previous task; stated dependencies override" (line 166) plus plan/0042's explicit-depends doctrine make parallel dispatch legitimate, so spec-obligations can be receipted with both lanes unbuilt. (2) Design intent: the #22 opening post carries the edge rationale verbatim — "- depends: series-lane, checklist-reporter (the spec models the shipped behaviour)" — and at that time the edges sufficed: the enumerated Kani targets were "area-prefix parser, include-path extractor, tab and trailing-whitespace detectors" (all reachable via series-lane) and mail-lane was thin ("format-patch parsing, subject-prefix coherence, cover-letter warn, transmission items attested"). The public-signals addendum then fattened mail-lane (MAINTAINERS parser, MIME, size cap, DMARC) and created forge-lane, both "depends on msg-lane", without revisiting spec-obligations' edges; the README merged the stale edge set while its own spec task promises "Kani harnesses for the byte-level predicates, proptest over open input spaces" and mail-lane's verify names exactly such a predicate: "the MAINTAINERS parser passes the live-syntax fixture set (three CC forms, the non-CC parenthesized address, the section-scoped duplicate globs)". (3) No later recovery: docs-and-release's verify is only "`host-lint --docs` clean; id-sync green; tag and assets present; #22 closed"; even though `host-lifecycle release` "runs the verify gate", a strict-discharge re-derivation verifies listed obligations only — it cannot flag lane code the manifest never modelled, and the plan states no spec-vs-lane-set completeness gate, so the staleness is silent as claimed. Two non-fatal imprecisions: the finding's evidence attributes the MAINTAINERS quote to both lanes when it sits in mail-lane's verify only (forge-lane's open-input surface is the PR-title grammar), and the remedy could also weigh project-pack-config's grammar checks (likewise unordered against spec-obligations), though that gap predates the merge and is outside this finding's claim. The two-edge remedy is minimal, matches the opening design's own rationale, and fixes the named defect. Severity major stands: not blocker (serial document-order execution avoids it; shipped code is not wrong), but a spec-discipline receipt that can green without the pack's largest byte-level parsers is a hole in the promised guarantee plus forced spec/manifest/harness rework — the plan/0052 no-hollow-green class the plan itself invokes.

survivors.claim: Network-dependent checks sit inside otherwise-offline, fixture-verified lanes with no tier distinction, no fail-closed semantics, no phase separation, and no fetch-failure verify coverage, so each can go hollow green: mail-lane's DMARC pre-send warn (a live _dmarc DNS TXT lookup) and patchwork post-send verification (live HTTP against patchwork.ffmpeg.org, temporally after the lane's pre-send run) appear in the task body but in none of its verify fixtures, with unstated behavior when DNS or patchwork is unreachable and no invocation surface separating post-send from pre-send; forge-lane's "delta comment on each push" exists only in the live PR timeline; and upstream-drift-lane specifies failure only on unacknowledged drift, never on a failed or denied fetch, names no endpoints or UA discipline — while the digest documents Anubis anti-bot gating denying AI-crawler and browser UAs "on HTML and API paths alike" on exactly these hosts. A swallowed DNS/HTTP failure or denied fetch reporting clean is the precise hollow green plan/0052 forbids; the plan's only network-isolation guarantee ("the offline gates never depend on this lane") covers the drift lane's consumers, not any of these checks' own failure semantics.
survivors.evidence: mail-lane: "the DMARC pre-send warn (a From: domain publishing reject or quarantine bounces off ffmpeg-devel), and patchwork post-send verification (the series appears, prior versions marked superseded)" — verify-by lists fixture cases for numbering, cover letter, oversize, HTML-only, wrong-list, hijacked-thread, missing-CC, but none for DMARC or patchwork; forge-lane: "force-push resubmit discipline (same PR per revision, no [PATCH vN] title, a delta comment on each push)" — verify-by covers title fixtures only; upstream-drift-lane: "Normalize, compare each rule-bearing section against the digest the corpus acknowledges, fail on unacknowledged drift" with verify "a doctored snapshot of each source fails the lane; acknowledging its digest passes it; the offline gates never depend on this lane" — nothing on fetch errors; digest: "Anubis denies browser-UA and AI-crawler-UA fetches, LLM-harness fetchers included, on HTML and API paths alike" vs ROUND2[3] "Anonymous read access to code.ffmpeg.org/api/v1 works today ... contradicting the assumption"; digest ROUND2[1]: "mechanically checkable via one DNS query at pre-send time".
survivors.remedy: Define fail-closed semantics per check: an unfetchable or denied drift source fails the lane (or renders unrun-red), an unanswerable DMARC or patchwork query renders the check unrun/warn and never clean. Split patchwork verification into an explicitly post-send, network-having step (own subcommand or flag); specify the drift lane's per-source fetch endpoints and UA discipline; give the network-having checks a named sub-tier or the receipts treatment; and add stubbed-DNS/stubbed-HTTP/fetch-failure cases to each verify line.
survivors.verify_evidence: Read plan/0072 README in full, issue #22 (all four bodies), issue #23, and the digest's mail-lane/Anubis/Forgejo sections. The core no-hollow-green claim is CONFIRMED on all three lanes. (1) mail-lane: the body carries "the DMARC pre-send warn (a From: domain publishing reject or quarantine bounces off ffmpeg-devel), and patchwork post-send verification (the series appears, prior versions marked superseded)" while its verify line lists only numbering/cover-letter/oversize/HTML-only/wrong-list/hijacked-thread/missing-CC fixtures plus the MAINTAINERS parser set — no DMARC, no patchwork, no fetch-failure case; the omission is load-bearing because the Build sequence preamble states "the verify lines here are the receipts' checks". Nothing upstream fills the gap: #22's addendum says only "the mail lane gains a post-send verification step (series visible on patchwork; prior versions marked superseded on resubmit)" and the digest only "mechanically checkable via one DNS query at pre-send time" — no failure semantics, no pre/post-send invocation surface anywhere. (2) forge-lane: "a delta comment on each push" sits in the README body (the addendum's forge-lane delta lists only "no new PR per revision, no [PATCH vN] title"; the delta comment is empirical observation, "usually with a delta comment"), and the verify fixtures are all title cases; same-PR/delta-comment discipline is checkable only against the live PR timeline, with unreachable-API behavior unstated. (3) upstream-drift-lane: "fail on unacknowledged drift" is the sole failure trigger and the verify line ("a doctored snapshot of each source fails the lane; acknowledging its digest passes it; the offline gates never depend on this lane") covers content drift only — a swallowed fetch error reporting clean is unguarded, the precise plan/0052 violation, and the settled-architecture no-hollow-green text covers only the mechanical/expensive/attested tiers, not these network checks. However, the finding's Anubis evidence is misattached: the digest's correction says "Anubis gates by User-Agent... plain non-browser clients pass through" and "Anonymous read access to code.ffmpeg.org/api/v1 works today... only the HTML UI paths (and trac.ffmpeg.org wiki pages) returned Anubis challenges" — the "on HTML and API paths alike" quote describes code.ffmpeg.org AI-crawler/browser UAs specifically. The drift lane's sources (developer.html, git-howto, security.html, mailing-list-faq.html, MAINTAINERS, fate.texi) live on ffmpeg.org and the git mirrors, none Anubis-gated, and the digest fetched all of them; so "on exactly these hosts" is false for the drift lane, and "names no endpoints" overstates (sources and pin keying are named; concrete fetch endpoints and failure semantics are not). The Anubis/UA-discipline concern is real but belongs to the forge lane's code.ffmpeg.org reads. Remedy is otherwise proportionate and uses the plan's own machinery (unrun-never-passed, receipts treatment, stubbed-DNS/HTTP fixtures); its "drift lane UA discipline" clause should move to the forge lane. Severity major stands: a hole in the promised no-hollow-green guarantee across three lanes, but the offline core gates are untouched.
survivors.corrected_claim: Network-dependent checks sit inside otherwise fixture-verified lanes with no tier assignment, no fail-closed semantics, and no fetch-failure verify coverage, so each can go hollow green: mail-lane's DMARC pre-send warn (a live _dmarc DNS TXT lookup) and patchwork post-send verification (live HTTP against patchwork.ffmpeg.org, temporally after the lane's pre-send directory scan) appear in the task body but in none of its verify fixtures, with unstated behavior when DNS or patchwork is unreachable and no invocation surface separating post-send from pre-send; forge-lane's same-PR/delta-comment resubmit discipline is checkable only against the live PR timeline (its verify fixtures are all title cases), on a host where the digest shows Anubis denying AI-crawler and browser UAs — API paths included — so its live re-check additionally needs stated UA discipline and denied-fetch semantics; and upstream-drift-lane specifies failure only on unacknowledged drift, never on a failed fetch of its (non-Anubis-gated, ffmpeg.org/git-mirror) sources, whose concrete fetch endpoints it leaves unnamed. A swallowed DNS/HTTP failure reporting clean is the hollow green plan/0052 forbids; the plan's only network-isolation guarantee ("the offline gates never depend on this lane") covers the drift lane's consumers, not any of these checks' own failure semantics, and the settled-architecture no-hollow-green tiers (mechanical/expensive/attested) assign these network checks no place.

survivors.claim: The security three-way routing classifier's core predicate — whether a fix addresses an exploitable issue versus non-exploitable UB or a leak — is a human security judgment no diff heuristic can decide, yet the plan assigns it no tier, no deciding authority, and no verdict semantics: the scope promises enforcement ("exploitable issues route to ffmpeg-security, never to the public patch paths"), series-lane lists the classifier among mechanical checks, the verify line quietly downgrades it to "the routing note" (exit 0, which cannot stop a public-path submission), the routing decision appears nowhere in checklist-reporter's attested set, nothing specifies what happens to a series carrying an exploitable-classified commit at mail/forge time, and the digest's stronger blocking shape was downgraded without record. For the consuming subtitle-decoder programme — which the scope itself expects to produce crash/overflow fixes — this is the live path with no usable design: executed as written it yields either a mechanical rule that false-greens exploitable fixes onto public paths (the worst possible wrong verdict for this pack) or a note dressed up as a routing guarantee.
survivors.evidence: Scope: "The security-path routing rules (exploitable issues route to ffmpeg-security, never to the public patch paths)"; series-lane: "the security three-way routing classifier (exploitable to ffmpeg-security, non-exploitable UB or leak to a Forgejo PR, everything else the normal path)" with no tier stated; series-lane verify: "a crash-fix commit with a new fate sample and no samples-request note fires both the routing note and the sample check"; checklist-reporter attested items: "named human reviewer, finder credit, reproducible-with-existing-applications" — no routing-decision leg. The digest's ROUND2[0] proposed the stronger shape — "blocks ordinary ffmpeg-devel/Forgejo submission for it" — and the downgrade to a note is nowhere recorded or argued.
survivors.remedy: Specify the mechanical part as a warn/note-tier trigger heuristic (crash/overflow/CVE signals suggest security review: "security-adjacent, routing undecided") and make the exploitability ruling a recorded, attested human disposition (project config or per-commit annotation) added as a checklist leg; a commit carrying a recorded exploitable disposition present in a mail or forge run is a flag; document the un-block motion (record a non-exploitable disposition); add verify cases for both directions; and reword the scope bullet so the pack does not promise to prevent public submission of exploitable fixes.
survivors.verify_evidence: Read the full README (plan/0072), all four layers of host-lint#22, and the digest's ROUND2[0]/critic sections. Every quoted anchor is accurate and the gap is real in both the README and the design record it transplants. (1) No tier or authority: README series-lane tiers every sibling public-signals addition ("the backport-fix focus warn", "sample minimality attested") but the classifier gets none — "the security three-way routing classifier (exploitable to ffmpeg-security, non-exploitable UB or leak to a Forgejo PR, everything else the normal path)"; issue #22's own build-sequence delta has the identical omission ("backport-fix focus warn; ... samples-request note ... minimal-sample attest; the security three-way routing classifier (exploitable, non-exploitable UB/leak, normal)"), so the README is not summarizing a tiered design held elsewhere. The tiers-freeze-at-calibration defense fails: the record's discipline is provisional-tier-then-calibrate ("Severity of the area-prefix rule is provisional until calibration"), and exploitability is not a false-positive-rate question calibration over accepted commits can decide. (2) The only verdict semantics anywhere is the verify line's "fires both the routing note and the sample check", and under the recorded exit semantics ("A missing receipt reports as a note; a stale one is a warn, exit 3, so a submission gate can require exit 0") a note is exit 0 and cannot stop a submission. (3) checklist-reporter's "security-path attested items (named human reviewer, finder credit, reproducible-with-existing-applications)" are security.html's report-contents items, which presuppose the routing decision rather than make it; no routing-disposition leg exists. (4) Re-read mail-lane and forge-lane in full: neither carries any security-routing item, so a series with an exploitable-classified commit passes both clean. (5) The digest downgrade is real and unrecorded: ROUND2[0] impact proposed a rule that "blocks ordinary ffmpeg-devel/Forgejo submission for it", the #22 addendum restates the upstream rule at full strength ("never to ffmpeg-devel or Forgejo as an ordinary patch"), and no document records or argues the note-tier landing. (6) Live path: the addendum itself says "A subtitle-decoder programme will plausibly produce crash/overflow fixes, so this routing is live for the consuming project", while the scope bullet promises "exploitable issues route to ffmpeg-security, never to the public patch paths" — a guarantee the note mechanism cannot honor, i.e. a no-hollow-green (plan/0052) violation by the plan's own doctrine. One loose phrase in the claim — "series-lane lists the classifier among mechanical checks" — is slightly imprecise (the lane also carries one attested item), but the classifier is presented as a firing check with a verdict, so the substance holds. The remedy is proportionate and matches the pack's three-tier architecture: mechanical trigger heuristic, attested human disposition as a checklist leg, flag at mail/forge time on a recorded exploitable disposition, bidirectional verify cases, and the scope reword; it correctly leaves the disposition home (config vs per-commit annotation) open. Severity major is right — not blocker, since the plan remains executable and the defect is confined to one rule area, but it is a hole in a promised guarantee whose failure mode is the worst wrong verdict this pack can produce.

survivors.claim: The pack's only contact with the consumer's real data — the frozen pgs8-wip reproduction of the four recorded findings (sign-off 4-of-29, missing lavu bump, five undocumented CLI options, shared-link failure) — is sequenced entirely after docs-and-release and the re-pin, so a reproduction failure (e.g. doc-updated as built not firing on the five CLI options, or the shared-abi/--enable-shared leg not surfacing the link failure) is discovered only in pgs-release and costs a full tool-carried release cycle to fix; every pre-release verify runs on synthetic fixtures or upstream commits, never on the series the pack exists to check.
survivors.evidence: Out of scope: "the acceptance runs (the live pgs9 series, plus the frozen pgs8-wip series as a known-findings regression fixture whose recorded mechanical findings the pack must reproduce). That is a pgs-release milestone, cut there once the release exists." docs-and-release verify: "`host-lint --docs` clean; id-sync green; tag and assets present; #22 closed" — no real-series run anywhere in the sequence. The pgs8-wip series is frozen (issue #22 addendum: "pgs8-wip freezes under its history tag"), so it is available and stable as a pre-release check target today.
survivors.remedy: Add a pre-release smoke reproduction to the sequence (a verify input of docs-and-release or a small task before it): run the built pack over the frozen pgs8-wip series locally and confirm all four recorded findings fire (signoff-all in submission mode, the avpriv version-bump, doc-updated, the shared-abi warn plus the --enable-shared receipted leg), keeping the formal receipted acceptance in pgs-release as scoped.
survivors.verify_evidence: Read the full README (/mnt/c/Users/dconnolly/Development/agentic-host/plan/0072-ffmpeg-commit-rule-pack/README.md), host-lint #23, and all of #22's comments. Every element of the finding checks out. (1) Sequencing: the README scopes out "the acceptance runs (the live pgs9 series, plus the frozen pgs8-wip series as a known-findings regression fixture whose recorded mechanical findings the pack must reproduce). That is a pgs-release milestone, cut there once the release exists" (lines 44-47), and #22's consolidated revision places "host-adoption (in pgs-release, not this repository)" with "depends: docs-and-release" and "pin bump" as its first step — the reproduction is behind the release and the pin. (2) No real-series contact pre-release: I walked every verify line in the build sequence; all run on synthetic fixtures, toy/stub repos, or upstream data (the "measured ground-truth subjects from #23" are 46 upstream subjects from a 2000-commit corpus; corpus-calibration runs "several thousand recent accepted upstream commits"). None touches pgs8-wip or pgs9. (3) Availability: #22's addendum states "pgs8-wip freezes under its history tag", so the fixture is stable and available today. (4) Cost: a post-release reproduction failure requires a pack fix shipped via `host-lifecycle release host-lint --change-class ...`, producer tag, agentic-host re-pin, and a pgs-release pin bump — a full tool-carried cycle per CLAUDE.md's dual-release-authority. The four findings match the record verbatim (#22 consolidated revision: "sign-off on 4 of 29, the missing lavu bump, the five undocumented CLI options, the shared-link failure via the `--enable-shared` leg"). Refutation angles tested and rejected: the synthetic fixtures do encode shapes *derived from* the four findings (e.g. series-lane's "an avpriv move without a minor bump flags"), but a fixture rendered from a finding is not the recorded series, and the encoding-mismatch scenario the finding names survives every pre-release gate; the deliberate scope boundary justifies deferring the receipted acceptance, not all contact with an available frozen ground-truth series. The remedy is feasible (corpus-calibration already establishes pre-release real-repo runs; submission mode is settable ad hoc; the branch-grammar operator decision is not needed for any of the four findings; no LGPL fixture-licensing implication since nothing is committed) and proportionate (it keeps the formal receipted acceptance in pgs-release as scoped). One refinement worth carrying into the fold-in: the shared-link reproduction via the `--enable-shared` leg requires actually building the frozen series, the heaviest of the four checks; the smoke task should treat the static shared-abi warn as the mandatory cheap check with the receipted build leg run once locally. Severity major stands: the plan's own guarantee ("must reproduce") is unverified until after the release that would have to be re-cut to fix it, but host-adoption does eventually catch the failure, so it is not a blocker.

survivors.claim: re-pin-and-receipt expects host-adoption to author "the pgs project pack TOML including the maintainer table the public-signals review recorded", but project-pack-config's TOML schema enumerates only upstream ref, mode, branch grammar, tag grammar, and frozen derivation — no maintainer-table, no unmaintained-path marking, no MAINTAINERS snapshot pin field — so at adoption time the consumer either cannot record the table or needs a schema change, which in this shop is another pack release plus re-pin.
survivors.evidence: project-pack-config: "TOML schema (upstream ref, mode, branch grammar, tag grammar, frozen derivation from history tags), the loader, a documented example, and the branch and tag checks"; re-pin-and-receipt: "the pgs project pack TOML including the maintainer table the public-signals review recorded". The digest (ROUND2[2]) specifies what the field must model: path-to-maintainer entries, the activity flag as "an optional enum {X,0,1,2,unflagged}", unmaintained paths (supdec.c/supenc.c/movtext*) passing clean, and "pin the parsed MAINTAINERS revision (commit sha + content hash) ... re-fetch/re-parse at each submission window".
survivors.remedy: Add the maintainer-table field (path glob scoped by library section, maintainer, CC marker, activity enum including unflagged, unmaintained marking) and the MAINTAINERS snapshot pin (SHA plus content hash) to the project-pack-config schema, with a precedence/parse test, so host-adoption is pure data authoring as intended.
survivors.verify_evidence: Read the README, issue #22 (body + all comments), and the digest ROUND2 MAINTAINERS section. The schema gap is real at every layer of the design record, not just in the README's summary. Opening post (project-pack-config): "TOML schema (upstream ref, mode, branch grammar, tag grammar, frozen derivation from history tags)... this task ships the schema, the loader, and a documented example" — no maintainer field. Consolidated revision: "As designed, with resolution corrected to worktree-first" — schema untouched. The public-signals addendum's build-sequence deltas amend eleven tasks but not project-pack-config, while its host-adoption delta states "the project config gains the maintainer table above" — the addendum amended the data-authoring task without amending the schema task the data must parse against, and the README transplants both sides of the contradiction verbatim (project-pack-config lists the same five elements; re-pin-and-receipt requires "the pgs project pack TOML including the maintainer table the public-signals review recorded"). No other task covers the field: mail-lane's MAINTAINERS parser parses the upstream file format, and rule-registry's "MAINTAINERS (snapshot pinned by SHA plus content hash)" is the encode-time registry pin, whereas the digest requires a project-config pin refreshed per submission window: "pin the parsed MAINTAINERS revision (commit sha + content hash) in the pgs-release project config and re-fetch/re-parse at each submission window, since the file changes multiple times per quarter." The digest also confirms the field shape the remedy names: activity flag "an optional enum {X, 0, 1, 2, unflagged}" recorded "in the pack's pgs-release project config"; "include these in the project config's path->maintainer table"; "the project config should mark these paths unmaintained" (supdec.c/supenc.c/movtext* passing clean on no-match). Refutations checked and failed: the enumeration is not non-exhaustive shorthand (it repeats the opening post's exhaustive statement, and project-pack-config's verify line tests only precedence/branch/tag/frozen — the maintainer table appears in no verify line anywhere); the table cannot be registry-resident (the addendum's own host-adoption delta and four digest impact lines place it in project config); the currently-empty mandatory-CC set does not defuse it (the table carries unmaintained markings and the re-fetch pin regardless, and a strict loader with parse tests would reject or silently drop an unmodeled table). Because re-pin-and-receipt depends on docs-and-release, the release ships before adoption begins, so the missing field forces a post-release loader change — under this shop's tool-carried release + dual-release-authority discipline, another pack release plus .host-software re-pin. Severity major is right (rework hole at the promised host-adoption seam, not an unsound verdict, so not blocker). The remedy is proportionate and matches the digest's field specification.

survivors.evidence: README: "- depends: everything above" (docs-and-release); "- depends: none (root)" (core-fail-closed-file-args, engine-surface, fixture-licensing). plan/0042 README: "A task may declare `depends`, a local `#anchor` or a cross-milestone `plan/NNNN#anchor`"; sibling plans enumerate anchors ("- depends: #skip-table-cells, #prose-quantity-operators, ..." in plan/0070; "- depends: none" in plan/0071).
survivors.remedy: Enumerate docs-and-release's actual prerequisites by task name (spec-obligations, checklist-reporter, hook-installer, upstream-drift-lane, project-pack-config, mail-lane, forge-lane, core-fix-release, fixture-licensing at minimum) and change the three roots to plain "none".
survivors.verify_evidence: Quoted text verified in plan/0072 README: line 433 "- depends: everything above" (docs-and-release), lines 175/203/223 "- depends: none (root)" (core-fail-closed-file-args, engine-surface, fixture-licensing). Grammar claim verified against plan/0042 README ("A task may declare `depends`, a local `#anchor` or a cross-milestone `plan/NNNN#anchor`") and empirically against the installed host-lifecycle: in a scratchpad fixture with anchored headings, `- depends: none` renders "depends: (root)" and the task joins the ready frontier, while `- depends: none (root)` and `- depends: everything above` render as unresolved literal text and the tasks are excluded from the frontier permanently ("ready frontier (deps satisfied...): plan/0001#task-b, plan/0002#gather-data" — the `none (root)` root task absent). So the two values are real grammar violations that wedge the graph. But the finding's mechanism clause is wrong: no HAZARD fires as written, because a prior defect hides the tasks entirely — every one of plan/0072's nineteen task headings lacks the `{#anchor}` suffix plan/0042 requires, and `host-lifecycle tasks` run on the real repo parses zero plan/0072 tasks, emitting per heading: "note: plan/0072-ffmpeg-commit-rule-pack/README.md:170: a `## Build sequence` task heading needs an anchor at its end (`### Title {#anchor}`), or a `- band` marker to make it a group" (all 19 headings, lines 170-436; plan/0071 is in the identical unparsed state, so citing it as the settled sibling is shaky — the tool-confirmed conformant sibling is plan/0070, `{#anchor}` headings with `#anchor` depends). The actual failure mode is worse than claimed: silent escape from the task graph and receipt discipline (no receipts ever demanded, no HAZARD), while the plan asserts "The tasks are anchored receipted nodes (plan/0042)". Remedy check: as given it is insufficient — fixing the depends values changes nothing until the `{#anchor}` suffixes are added to all headings; depends refs should use the plan/0042 `#anchor` form, not bare names; and the enumerated docs-and-release prerequisite list omits pack-dispatch, the one leaf task nothing else depends on (core-fix-release, spec-obligations, mail-lane, forge-lane, hook-installer, upstream-drift-lane cover the rest transitively), so under the proposed remedy the release would never gate on the pack dispatch verb.
survivors.corrected_claim: plan/0072's build sequence is outside the plan/0042 task grammar in two compounding ways: (1) none of its nineteen `###` task headings carries the required `{#anchor}` suffix, so host-lifecycle parses zero tasks from the plan (each heading draws only a parser note) and the milestone silently escapes the task graph and per-task receipt gate entirely — no HAZARD fires, contradicting the plan's own "The tasks are anchored receipted nodes (plan/0042)"; and (2) once anchored, two depends values remain unresolvable — "everything above" (docs-and-release) and "none (root)" (three roots) parse as literal dependency text that permanently blocks those tasks and everything downstream (empirically confirmed against the installed tool; plain "none" is the accepted root form, per the tool and plan/0070, the one tool-conformant sibling). The remedy must add the heading anchors, use `#anchor`-form depends, replace "none (root)" with "none", and enumerate docs-and-release's prerequisites including pack-dispatch, which is otherwise a leaf the release never waits on.

survivors.claim: The plan's handling of the five open operator decisions fails in both directions. Decision 5 (calibration threshold) "has no recommendation; it is set when the calibration report exists", so the proceed-on-recommendations escape cannot apply to it, yet corpus-calibration's verify needs "the agreed rate" — a hidden mid-task operator ruling with no node or edge in the graph, blocking series-lane and the entire critical path on an operator action the graph never names. Meanwhile the blanket claim "each ruling lands in the registry or config as data, not as a redesign" is false for decision 1 (pack residence): an adverse residence ruling is precisely a redesign (workspace-split, release assets, and docs-and-release all restructure), and the repo state has already foreclosed it — the workspace split and pack skeleton landed on host-lint main (3faa4c0b) before any recorded ruling — so the plan presents as open a phantom decision its own executed roots have decided. The research record itself says the milestone "should carry these five as explicit pre-task or in-task decision points".
survivors.evidence: README: "The build sequence proceeds on the recommendations until the operator rules otherwise; each ruling lands in the registry or config as data, not as a redesign. 1. Pack residence. Recommended: the host-lint cargo workspace..."; "5. The calibration threshold ... This one has no recommendation; it is set when the calibration report exists"; corpus-calibration verify: "a gate test asserts no flag-tier mechanical rule exceeds the agreed rate on the calibration corpus"; "### series-lane ... depends: cosmetic-separation, corpus-calibration". host-lint main commit 3faa4c0b "split the repo into a workspace and add the ffmpeg pack skeleton" (confirmed via the API and #23's comment). signals-digest.md (host-lint-open-reports): "the milestone should carry these five as explicit pre-task or in-task decision points".
survivors.remedy: Add an explicit operator-gated node (calibration report committed → operator rules the rate, recorded as a call/ decision → tier-freeze gate) between the report and the freeze, so the receipt records the ruling as plan/0042 anchored-node doctrine expects and the mid-task wait is visible in the graph. Mark decision 1 as executed-on-recommendation with the revisit trigger (a second pack) recorded, and scope the "as data, not as a redesign" sentence to decisions 2–4.

survivors.claim: msg-lane lands real upstream text in the repo ("the measured ground-truth subjects from #23 join the fixtures as a must-pass corpus") but depends only on rule-registry, while fixture-licensing's own text requires the policy "decided before any fixture lands" and only diff-lane was given the edge; since stated dependencies override document order, the graph as written permits real-excerpt fixtures to land before the licensing policy exists. rule-registry (which carries fixture entries for the widened sources) has the same gap, and corpus-calibration ingests several thousand real upstream commits with no fixture-licensing edge if any corpus snapshot is retained for its committed gate test.
survivors.evidence: "### msg-lane ... depends: rule-registry — verify by: the measured ground-truth subjects from #23 join the fixtures as a must-pass corpus" versus "### diff-lane ... depends: rule-registry, fixture-licensing"; fixture-licensing: "The licensing policy decided before any fixture lands" and "a real upstream excerpt only where synthesis cannot reproduce the case, isolated with explicit provenance and licensing notes ... a CI check requires it wherever real excerpts sit"; preamble: "Tasks with no stated dependency follow the previous task; stated dependencies override."
survivors.remedy: Add fixture-licensing to msg-lane's depends (and to rule-registry, which carries fixture entries for the widened sources), and state in corpus-calibration whether the calibration corpus is fetched-at-run or retained in-repo — if retained, add the fixture-licensing edge there too.
survivors.verify_evidence: Read plan/0072-ffmpeg-commit-rule-pack/README.md in full, host-lint #22 (all comments, including the consolidated revision) and #23 via gh, and scanned signals-digest.md for licensing/retention statements. All load-bearing quotes check out. README preamble: "Tasks with no stated dependency follow the previous task; stated dependencies override" — so msg-lane's "depends: rule-registry" is its only ordering constraint, and core-fail-closed → engine-surface → workspace-split → rule-registry → msg-lane is a valid topological order that never executes fixture-licensing. msg-lane's fixtures are unambiguously real upstream excerpts: #22's consolidated revision reads "the 46 measured ground-truth subjects from #23 join the fixtures as a must-pass corpus," and #23 shows these are verbatim subjects measured from "subject corpora of 2000 and 5000 recent upstream commits" (e.g. `Bump versions for master after release/8.1`). fixture-licensing's own text — "The licensing policy decided before any fixture lands" and the CI check "wherever real excerpts sit" — puts that corpus in scope, yet only diff-lane carries the edge ("depends: rule-registry, fixture-licensing"). Refutation attempts fail: document order does not bind a task with stated deps; and while #23 originally scoped the decision to diff-lane for LGPL code excerpts ("Decide before diff-lane lands, because retrofitting a fixture corpus is rework"), the README broadened the policy to "any fixture" while keeping only the diff-lane edge, so the contradiction is the plan's own. The gap also originates verbatim in #22's consolidated revision (identical depends lines) — the README transplanted it faithfully, which does not excuse the plan. Two caveats that do not flip the verdict: (1) the rule-registry leg is asserted as fact but is inference — #22 says the widened sources come "each with per-section digests and fixture entries," and an entry is a mapping-plus-digest, not necessarily retained upstream text, so that instance is plausible rather than demonstrated; (2) the corpus-calibration leg is correctly conditional — the verify line's gate test "asserts ... on the calibration corpus" while "the offline gates never depend on" the network lane, and no task states whether the corpus is retained, a real unstated ambiguity. The remedy is proportionate: fixture-licensing is a cheap root task, the msg-lane edge is the load-bearing fix and transitively covers mail-lane (whose "live-syntax fixture set" of real MAINTAINERS forms depends only on msg-lane) and forge-lane; the rule-registry edge is belt-and-braces. Severity minor is right: one-edge rework, contained, but executing the graph as written can land real-excerpt fixtures before the policy exists, the exact retrofit rework #23 warned against.

survivors.evidence: README msg-lane: "the `Revert`/`fixup!`/`squash!` exemptions"; consolidated revision: "`Revert` is exempt in every mode, `fixup!` and `squash!` in wip mode"; #23 remedy: "exempt `Revert`, `fixup!`, and `squash!` shapes (the latter two in wip mode)".

survivors.verify_evidence: All three quotes verified verbatim. README msg-lane (plan/0072 README line 247) lists only "the `Revert`/`fixup!`/`squash!` exemptions" with no mode scoping, while the same sentence writes "mode-aware sign-off" — so the README carries mode qualifiers where intended, and the unqualified exemptions read as unconditional; no other README section restores the scoping. #22's consolidated revision msg-lane task reads: "`Revert` is exempt in every mode, `fixup!` and `squash!` in wip mode". #23's area-prefix remedy reads: "exempt `Revert`, `fixup!`, and `squash!` shapes (the latter two in wip mode)". The failure scenario is live because nothing in the stated verify lines would catch an unconditional implementation: the must-pass ground-truth corpus is drawn from landed upstream master subjects, which cannot contain fixup!/squash! shapes (they are autosquashed before landing), and neither the README's verify line nor the consolidated revision's mandates a mode-dimension fixture. Containment (why minor, not major, is correct): the README's preamble delegates rule bodies to the issue record ("the issues are the record") and each task names its transplant source, so an implementer working from the record gets the scoping right — but the README also presents its build sequence as the merged "one final sequence," making the lossy restatement operative plan text. The remedy is proportionate: restoring the scoping is a three-word edit, and the submission-mode fixup!-flags fixture is a genuine strengthening that pins a dimension not even the record's verify line tested.

survivors.verify_evidence: All three quotes verified verbatim. README diff-lane (plan/0072-ffmpeg-commit-rule-pack/README.md line 261) reads "the lexical VLA rule retired in favour of a `-Wvla` build leg" with no parenthetical, and `grep -i complex` over the whole README exits 1 — the complex-number half appears nowhere in the milestone document, including the diff-lane verify line ("a `*.mak` tab, a `tests/ref` golden file, and an fftools identifier"), which fixtures every other exemption but no complex.h case. Defenses fail: the README's "the issues are the record" disclaimer does not cover this, because the diff-lane paragraph is a detailed restatement carrying every other element of the consolidated task plus the public-signals additions, and the README declares its task graph the merged "one final sequence"; and the completeness-as-a-test fixture only requires a registry mapping per rule-bearing section, which the expensive `-Wvla` entry can satisfy while the complex-number lexical check vanishes (`-Wvla` catches VLAs, not complex numbers). One overstatement that does not change the verdict: the "re-tier as expensive" sub-claim is substantively covered elsewhere — the README's "in favour of a `-Wvla` build leg" plus build-receipts explicitly listing `-Wvla` among the receipted legs conveys the re-tier; only the registry-metadata phrasing is missing. The load-bearing defect (the dropped complex-number lexical half and its absent fixtures) is real; severity minor is right (one contained rule regression) and the remedy is correct and proportionate.
