survivors.claim: The public-signals promotion of SI-units from attested to a mechanical advisory (including the attached prefix-unit form) is named only at the registry level; no lane task implements the check and no verify fixture covers it, so the promoted rule has nowhere to run and risks staying attested-in-practice or landing unspecified mid-build.
survivors.evidence: README: "The SI-units item became written rule text and promotes from attested to advisory" and rule-registry absorbs "SI-units promotion", but diff-lane's enumerated additions are only "the narrow-variable-scope advisory heuristic ... and the self-describing AVOption constant heuristic"; the addendum specifies "a mechanical advisory, including the attached prefix-unit form ('centiseconds', never 'centi seconds')".
survivors.remedy: Name the implementing lane (diff-lane advisory over added lines/comments/docs) with a prefix-unit fixture in its verify line.
survivors.verify_evidence: The defect is real and the finding's quotes are accurate. Exhaustive search for SI-units across the plan and the design record shows the promotion is named only at registry level and nowhere given an implementing check or fixture. README (/mnt/c/Users/dconnolly/Development/agentic-host/plan/0072-ffmpeg-commit-rule-pack/README.md): line 112 "The SI-units item became written rule text and promotes from attested to advisory"; line 232 rule-registry absorbs "SI-units promotion"; those are the only SI-units mentions — diff-lane enumerates only "the narrow-variable-scope advisory heuristic ... and the self-describing AVOption constant heuristic", series-lane's "public-signals additions land here too" list is backport-fix warn, fate-sample checks, and security routing, and no verify line anywhere mentions SI units or the prefix-unit form (the only "prefix" hits are subject-prefix/area-prefix, unrelated). The gap traces to the design record itself: the #22 public-signals addendum specifies "the SI-units entry promotes from attested to a mechanical advisory, including the attached prefix-unit form (\"centiseconds\", never \"centi seconds\")", yet its own Build-sequence deltas assign SI-units only to rule-registry while giving the same drift-set's narrow-scope item an explicit diff-lane delta — so the README's merge faithfully reproduced an unassigned rule. Two considered refutations fail: (1) "the registry is the implementing home" — the registry task builds the corpus encoding and `rules`/`rules --json`/`rules --verify-source` surfaces, while every check is implemented in a named lane, and the settled-architecture tier definition says mechanical means "the pack asserts it", which nothing here asserts; (2) "completeness-as-a-test catches it" — the completeness fixture only fails "when any entry lacks a registry mapping", and si-units already holds a mapping from the consolidated revision, so the test passes with the rule unimplemented. The finding is in fact slightly conservative: the consolidated revision's checklist-reporter, which the README's checklist-reporter inherits without re-enumerating, still lists si-units in "the widened attested set (sample-provenance, valgrind-asan, warnings-policy, si-units, vertical-alignment)", so the merged plan carries a latent contradiction — registry says advisory, reporter still renders it attested — which is exactly the "attested-in-practice" outcome the claim predicts. Remedy check: naming diff-lane as the implementing lane with a prefix-unit fixture is proportionate and matches how the same commit-set's narrow-scope heuristic was handled; the remedy should additionally drop si-units from checklist-reporter's inherited attested enumeration so the report tier matches the registry tier. Severity minor is right: one advisory-tier rule, contained, caught at build time, but real rework if the registry entry freezes before an implementing lane is named.

survivors.claim: The record bins five security.html report-contents items as mechanically checkable (commit-hash source identifier, reproducible command-line testcase, stack trace with line numbers, git-formatted patch, uniquely named attachments) plus the CVE administrivia routing, but the plan lands only the three-way routing classifier (series-lane) and the three attested items (checklist-reporter) — the mechanical majority of the security-report surface is neither delivered nor marked deferred, the exact "silently absent" state the plan's own out-of-scope discipline (patcheck entries "deferred rather than silently absent") forbids. Since rule-registry puts every rule-bearing security.html section in the completeness fixture, execution either fails the completeness gate or forces an unplanned scope decision; and when the consumer's first exploitable fix needs a report, the pack checks nothing about it.
survivors.evidence: Addendum: "The report contents are a 10-item list ... mechanically checkable items (git commit hash source identifier, reproducible command-line testcase, stack trace with line numbers, git-formatted patch, uniquely named attachments)" and "CVE administrivia routes to ffmpeg-cve@ffmpeg.org pre-backport"; digest ROUND2[0]: "the security-report checker should mechanically verify the checkable items ... and attest the human ones"; README lands only "the security three-way routing classifier" (series-lane) and "the security-path attested items (named human reviewer, finder credit, reproducible-with-existing-applications)" (checklist-reporter), while rule-registry claims "every rule-bearing chapter sits in the completeness fixture".
survivors.remedy: Either add the five mechanical report checks as a small security-report leg in a named task (a checklist section or security-report subcommand), or record them and the ffmpeg-cve routing as explicit deferred registry entries mapped by the completeness fixture's security.html section.
survivors.verify_evidence: Read plan/0072 README in full, issue #22 with comments (security.html section and Build-sequence deltas of the public-signals addendum), and digest ROUND2[0]. The quotes are accurate: the addendum bins the 10-item report contents into "mechanically checkable items (git commit hash source identifier, reproducible command-line testcase, stack trace with line numbers, git-formatted patch, uniquely named attachments) and attested items", records "CVE administrivia routes to ffmpeg-cve@ffmpeg.org pre-backport", and the digest's impact line says "the security-report checker should mechanically verify the checkable items ... and attest the human ones". Exhaustive grep of the README confirms the only security-path deliveries are series-lane's "security three-way routing classifier" and checklist-reporter's three "security-path attested items"; the ffmpeg-cve routing leg and all five mechanical report checks appear nowhere in any task, and the out-of-scope section — which explicitly defers patcheck ("deferred rather than silently absent") and carries release-manager exclusions "as explicit entries" — lists neither, so the plan's own discipline is violated. The completeness consequence holds: rule-registry widens the fixture to security.html per section and verifies "the completeness test fails when any rule-bearing chapter entry lacks a registry mapping" — the CVE section has no mapping at all (gate fails or forces an unplanned scope decision), and the report-contents section maps only via the attested items (the five mechanical items go silently absent). Two caveats for the record, neither refuting: (1) the omission originates in the design record itself — the addendum's own Build-sequence deltas list also dropped the digest's mechanical binning, so the README transplanted an upstream gap rather than introducing one, but the milestone is where scope must be settled and it neither lands nor defers the items; (2) "the pack checks nothing about it" is mildly overstated — the routing classifier fires on an exploitable fix and three attested items render on the checklist — but the report's mechanical contents are genuinely unchecked. Severity minor is right (contained rework: one small leg or a handful of deferred registry entries), and the remedy is proportionate and matches the plan's existing patcheck-deferral pattern.

survivors.claim: forge-lane makes "a delta comment on each push" part of the enforced resubmit discipline, but the record establishes it only as an observed usual practice, not a rule, and the addendum's delta names only same-PR and no-[PATCH vN]-title as the discipline; a check requiring the comment would flag accepted upstream practice.
survivors.evidence: README forge-lane: "force-push resubmit discipline (same PR per revision, no [PATCH vN] title, a delta comment on each push)"; addendum: "Resubmission is a force-push to the same PR, usually with a delta comment" and delta list: "force-push resubmit discipline (no new PR per revision, no [PATCH vN] title)"; digest: "vN resubmission on the forge is expressed as force-pushes to the same PR, usually with an explanatory comment".
survivors.remedy: Demote the delta-comment element to an advisory or drop it from the discipline check, matching the record's "usually" (this also resolves its live-PR-timeline observability problem noted in the network-checks finding).
survivors.verify_evidence: Read /mnt/c/Users/dconnolly/Development/agentic-host/plan/0072-ffmpeg-commit-rule-pack/README.md (forge-lane task), the full issue #22 comment thread (gh issue view 22 --comments), and the resubmission sections of the signals digest. All quoted texts verified. README forge-lane: "force-push resubmit discipline (same PR per revision, no [PATCH vN] title, a delta comment on each push)". Public-signals addendum on #22 states the fact as "Resubmission is a force-push to the same PR, usually with a delta comment; never a new PR, never [PATCH vN] in the title", and its forge-lane delta names the discipline as only "force-push resubmit discipline (no new PR per revision, no [PATCH vN] title)" — no comment element. Digest (lb=True finding): "vN resubmission on the forge is expressed as force-pushes to the same PR, usually with an explanatory comment", with evidence showing merged PR 23440 had five force-pushes with no comments recorded and only PR 23080's single force-push carrying a quoted delta comment — so a per-push comment requirement would flag accepted upstream practice (23440 merged). One complicating datum weighed and rejected as a defense: the digest's impact line does say "encode instead: resubmit = force-push to the same PR with a comment describing the delta", but that impact line exceeds the digest's own "usually" evidence, and the addendum — the later, curated record the README itself declares authoritative ("the issues are the record") — dropped the comment from the discipline list. Corroborating: the README's forge-lane verify line (fixture PR metadata cases: bare-branch title, area-prefixed title, WIP title, versioned title) contains no delta-comment fixture, so the elevated requirement is also unverified as written. The remedy (demote to advisory or drop, matching "usually") is proportionate; severity minor is correct — one sub-element of one lane, contained fix.

survivors.claim: The README's transplant of the backport-fix focus rule drops the public-signals addendum's honesty qualifier "the judgment core stays attested": deciding which hunks "the fix does not need" is a judgment call the addendum explicitly kept attested, but the plan states it as a bare mechanical warn with no fixture in the series-lane verify line — a mechanical rule whose predicate is not mechanically decidable, contradicting the plan's own tier discipline; an executing agent reading the README's task text (declared authoritative for the receipts' checks) could implement the warn beyond its decidable scope.
survivors.evidence: Plan series-lane: "the backport-fix focus warn (a ticket- or CVE-referencing fix carrying hunks the fix does not need warns to split)"; #22 public-signals addendum: "a commit identified as a bug fix (ticket/CVE reference present) that also carries hunks not needed for the fix warns to split; the judgment core stays attested"; digest ANGLE ffmpeg-submission-process: "advisory tier, cannot be fully mechanical" / "not mechanically decidable, so attested-tier".
survivors.remedy: Restore the qualifier in the series-lane task text (mechanical trigger: bug-fix reference plus multi-concern diff heuristic; judgment core attested) and add a positive/negative fixture pair to the verify line.
survivors.verify_evidence: Verified all three quoted sources verbatim. README series-lane: "the backport-fix focus warn (a ticket- or CVE-referencing fix carrying hunks the fix does not need warns to split)" — qualifier absent. Issue #22 public-signals addendum (drift entry for e0a012d12990): "...warns to split; the judgment core stays attested." Digest line 62: "advisory tier, cannot be fully mechanical"; line 146: "not mechanically decidable, so attested-tier"; line 455: "at minimum add it to the attested checklist." The drop is material, not covered elsewhere: (a) the README declares its build sequence "one final sequence" and "the verify lines here are the receipts' checks," so the compressed restatement is what executes, and the same sentence preserves the sibling attested qualifier ("sample minimality attested"), showing this is transplant loss, not a compression convention; (b) no verify line catches it — the completeness fixture checks only that the developer.texi section has a registry mapping (a bare mechanical warn satisfies it; tier composition untested), and the checklist-reporter grep guards only attested items that exist in the registry, so a never-encoded attested leg is invisible; the addendum's checklist-reporter delta lists other new attested legs but not this one, so the qualifier's only home was the sentence the README truncated. Remedy is proportionate on its main half (restore the qualifier in the task text); severity minor is correct (warn-tier rule, issue record retains the qualifier, one-clause fix).
survivors.corrected_claim: As stated, except one sub-claim is overstated: the series-lane verify line is not entirely fixture-silent — its generic clause "the synthetic-repo suite extends per rule" mandates coverage for the warn; what is missing is a named fixture (the sibling public-signals additions get one) and, more importantly, any check that the attested judgment core exists at all, which no fixture pair can supply — the remedy's fixture half pins only the mechanical trigger's decidable boundary, while restoring the attested clause in the task text/registry is the load-bearing fix.

survivors.claim: The fate-sample checks hinge on knowing fate-suite contents ("a sample absent from fate-suite", "an existing fate-suite path is never repurposed"), but the plan never says where that state comes from at run time (a local synced fate-suite tree is tens of GB; a manifest would need pinning like MAINTAINERS) nor what the check reports in a target clone with no fate-suite present — if it passes clean when it cannot consult the suite, it is a hollow green; if it needs the tree, the check is silently expensive/networked, not mechanical.
survivors.evidence: series-lane: "the fate-sample checks (a FATE test referencing a sample absent from fate-suite requires a samples-request note, an existing fate-suite path is never repurposed, sample minimality attested)"; verify uses only "a crash-fix commit with a new fate sample and no samples-request note" against a synthetic repo — no case for the suite-unavailable state.
survivors.remedy: Name the fate-suite source (local FATE_SAMPLES tree when configured, else a pinned path manifest in the registry) and mandate that with neither available the check renders unrun/note, never pass; add that case to the verify line.
survivors.verify_evidence: The underspecification is real at every layer. README series-lane words the checks as suite-state predicates — "the fate-sample checks (a FATE test referencing a sample absent from fate-suite requires a samples-request note, an existing fate-suite path is never repurposed, sample minimality attested)" — and its verify line covers only "a crash-fix commit with a new fate sample and no samples-request note", no suite-unavailable case. The delegated record does not fill the gap: issue #22's public-signals addendum says only "the series-lane check requires a samples-request note for any commit whose FATE test references a sample path absent from fate-suite", and the digest (fate.texi findings) says "references a sample path not already in fate-suite" — neither names a runtime source. The project-pack-config TOML schema ("upstream ref, mode, branch grammar, tag grammar, frozen derivation from history tags") has no samples-path field, and the settled-architecture no-hollow-green bullet articulates degraded-state rendering only for the expensive/attested tiers, while these checks land as mechanical series-lane additions. BUT the finding's dilemma ("hollow green" or "silently expensive/networked") is not exhaustive, and that weakens both the claim and the remedy: the plan's own verify fixture is "the synthetic-repo suite" — a repo with no fate-suite at all — where the check is nonetheless expected to fire "sample absent", which only works under a tree-local proxy semantics (a sample path is new iff unreferenced at the series base; a samples-request note targeting an already-referenced path flags as repurposing). The digest's own gloss "new samples must be new paths" (impact line on the never-replace rule) supports exactly this mechanical, cheap reading. So the checks do not necessarily "hinge on knowing fate-suite contents"; the defect is that the plan never says which semantics is meant. Remedy caveat: the proposed "pinned path manifest in the registry" would be a large, drifting artifact needing its own pinning discipline and is disproportionate; the right fix is a one-sentence definition of the tree-local proxy as the data source (with the unrun/note-never-pass rendering and a verify case required only if any variant consults external suite state, e.g. an optional FATE_SAMPLES leg). Severity minor is correct for the ambiguity as it stands.
survivors.corrected_claim: The fate-sample checks' data source is unspecified across all three record layers: "absent from fate-suite" and "an existing fate-suite path" name state the pack has no stated way to obtain, no config field supplies a samples tree, and no verify case covers the checks' behavior when that state is unobtainable. The gap is ambiguity, not a forced bad choice: a mechanical tree-local semantics (a sample path is new iff unreferenced at the series base; a samples-request note naming an already-referenced path flags as repurposing) is implied by the plan's own synthetic-repo verify line and the digest's "new samples must be new paths", but is nowhere stated, so an implementer could equally build a suite-consulting variant that either silently passes without the suite (hollow green, violating plan/0052) or acquires an undeclared multi-GB/network dependency inside a mechanical lane. Fix: define the tree-local proxy as the check's data source in the series-lane task; if any variant consults an actual fate-suite tree (e.g. optional FATE_SAMPLES), mandate unrun/note rendering when absent — never pass — and add that case to the verify line. A pinned path manifest of the suite is not warranted.

survivors.claim: rule-registry's parenthetical mislabels "the five-commit developer.texi drift set": the actual five commits are the whitespace relaxation (3cfbf561), narrow-scope (0c6b4ad5), SI typo (2aad4fb2), backport-minimal (e0a012d1), and reviews/Fairy (01a9a98f) — but the plan's list swaps in "tracker transition" (commit a5ee6ff7, which touches issue_tracker.txt/infra.txt/MAINTAINERS, not developer.texi), omits the relaxation, and lists "SI-units promotion" whose substantive rule text (20cade1b) sits inside the pinned blob. An encode step or drift fixture built from this list acknowledges the wrong hunk set.
survivors.evidence: Plan: "the five-commit developer.texi drift set is absorbed (narrow-scope, backport-minimal, SI-units promotion, reviews/Fairy, tracker transition)"; digest: "Exactly five upstream master commits changed doc/developer.texi after the pinned file state ... 3cfbf56192fb (2026-03-25), 0c6b4ad5fcac (2026-04-22), 2aad4fb2e37c (2026-05-16), e0a012d12990 (2026-06-24), 01a9a98f353b (2026-07-13)" and "commit a5ee6ff7209e ... changes doc/issue_tracker.txt".
survivors.remedy: Correct the parenthetical to the five actual developer.texi commits and list the tracker transition and SI-units promotion as separate absorbed items with their own sources.
survivors.verify_evidence: Read plan/0072 README rule-registry task (lines 230-232) and the signals digest's drift-enumeration sections. The plan states: "the five-commit developer.texi drift set is absorbed (narrow-scope, backport-minimal, SI-units promotion, reviews/Fairy, tracker transition)" — a five-item parenthetical anchored to "five-commit developer.texi drift set", so it enumerates that set. The digest (verified, lb=False) states: "Exactly five upstream master commits changed doc/developer.texi after the pinned file state, and no others ...: 3cfbf56192fb (2026-03-25), 0c6b4ad5fcac (2026-04-22), 2aad4fb2e37c (2026-05-16), e0a012d12990 (2026-06-24), 01a9a98f353b (2026-07-13)", with 3cfbf561 identified as "whitespace relaxation, already in corpus" and 2aad4fb2 as "typo only". The tracker transition is a different commit outside the set: "commit a5ee6ff7209e ... changes doc/issue_tracker.txt ... doc/infra.txt ... and marks the Trac bug tracker '[X]' ... in MAINTAINERS ... developer.texi's own commit-message wording is tracker-neutral and unchanged." And the SI-units substance predates the pin: "the SI-units rule itself (20cade1ba34a, 2026-03-06) is already inside the pinned blob and encoded as attested in the corpus." So two of the five listed items (tracker transition, SI-units promotion) are not members of the developer.texi drift set, and one actual member (the 3cfbf561 whitespace relaxation) is omitted — exactly as the finding claims. The one charitable defense — that the parenthetical lists absorption work items, under which omitting the already-in-corpus relaxation is defensible — fails because the plan explicitly labels the list as the five-commit set. Severity minor is correct: the plan's public-signals review section states the underlying facts correctly and each substantive item is handled somewhere (relaxation in cosmetic-separation's fixtures, tracker transition in msg-lane's ticket-ref, SI promotion in the review narrative), so no wrong rule ships; but rule-registry's verify line keys a freshness check to "the newest developer.texi commit" and drift fixtures to per-section digests, and an encoder working from the mislabeled parenthetical would attribute non-developer.texi hunks (issue_tracker.txt/infra.txt/MAINTAINERS) to the texi drift set and have no listed source for the relaxation hunk — contained rework, real. The remedy (name the five actual commits; carry tracker-transition and SI-promotion as separately-sourced absorbed items) is proportionate and matches the digest's own partition ("Sibling drift outside developer.texi: the issue tracker transitioned").

survivors.claim: The README asserts the security page "changed five times in 2026 alone"; the live FFmpeg/web history shows 11 commits touching src/security in 2026, and even the digest's own dated enumeration names six policy commits, so the count as stated matches neither the source nor the record.

Live `repos/FFmpeg/web/commits?path=src/security&since=2026-01-01`: 11 commits (207ade09c4 2026-02-04 through bb6e9eabd7, committed 2026-07-04). survivors.verify_evidence: README line 403 (upstream-drift-lane) states verbatim: "plus security.html (keyed to FFmpeg/web commits, since the page changed five times in 2026 alone)". A fresh gh query on 2026-07-17 (`repos/FFmpeg/web/commits?path=src/security&since=2026-01-01`) returns exactly 11 commits, 207ade09c4 (2026-02-04) through bb6e9eabd7 (committed 2026-07-04), five of them CVE-list housekeeping (af729860df + revert c50553e6c2, b5e2422fc7, ceedb630be, 9f62830b24). The digest's dated lb-claim entries name six policy commits (207ade09c4, 7d77d5eef5, e75c253246, 38abe60f82, e2fec87df2, bb6e9eabd7), while its "changed five times in 2026 alone" phrase appears only once, in the impact note of the no-SECURITY-file claim ("re-check security.html on corpus refresh since the page changed five times in 2026 alone") — the README copied that unverified aside verbatim rather than the digest's own evidence-bearing enumeration. So the unqualified statement is wrong against the live record (11) and unsupported by the digest's enumeration (6). Severity minor is right: the count is a rationale clause, the lane design (keyed to FFmpeg/web commits, per-section comparison at README lines 404-405) is unaffected. One remedy caveat: the proposed replacement wording "five policy-bearing commits" contradicts the finding's own six-commit enumeration (the delta is whether 38abe60f82, the Forgejo link fix, is policy-bearing); the fix should say six per the digest's dated enumeration or state the raw record (11 commits, roughly half CVE-list housekeeping) rather than re-introduce a contestable count.

survivors.claim: After the first properly-linked mentions, the README body uses bare "#22"/"#23" roughly a dozen times — including inside verify lines — violating the operator-recorded doctrine that an issue reference must sit in a markdown link to the full GitHub URL; in the published mdBook site a bare "#23" is ambiguous between host-lint's and this repo's trackers.
survivors.evidence: README: "Every design-level finding of #23 is folded into #22's consolidated build sequence"; "The consolidated revision on #22 settled these after #23's review"; "the measured ground-truth subjects from #23"; verify lines "#23 closed", "#22 closed"; "Close #22." PLAN.md follow-up table: "Enforce full GitHub URLs in place of bare `#N` issue references (a bare `#N` is not a safe full URL) ... plan/0049 added that the reference must sit in a markdown link to the full URL, so it renders in the mdBook site."

survivors.verify_evidence: The factual inventory is accurate: grep over /mnt/c/Users/dconnolly/Development/agentic-host/plan/0072-ffmpeg-commit-rule-pack/README.md shows first mentions properly linked (lines 9, 12, 105, 156, 159 all use [host-lint#22]/[host-lint#23] with full URLs) followed by 15 bare occurrences (lines 13, 26, 55, 138, 164, 167, 180, 182, 185, 229, 254, 431, 434), including the two verify lines "#23 closed" (line 185) and "#22 closed" (line 434). The PLAN.md quote is also accurate. But the "violating the operator-recorded doctrine" framing overstates the doctrine's status: the deciding text in PLAN.md line 20 is the follow-up row's status column — "Enforce full GitHub URLs in place of bare `#N` issue references (a bare `#N` is not a safe full URL) | operator, plan/0042 review | **Future plan, not yet cut.** plan/0049 added that the reference must sit in a markdown link to the full URL" — i.e. the rule is a recorded, queued enforcement obligation, not in-force governance, and bare refs are corpus-wide recorded debt (plan/0001, 0002, 0004, 0005, 0022, 0023, 0024, 0026 READMEs all carry them). Decisively, PLAN.md's own freshly-committed index row for plan/0072 (line 170) uses the identical link-first-then-bare pattern: "...and [host-lint#23](https://github.com/connollydavid/host-lint/issues/23) (#23 stays open for the live core defect...)" — so the README matches current committed practice in the very index the review calls binding. The ambiguity harm is also weaker than claimed: `gh issue view 22|23 --repo connollydavid/agentic-host` returns "Could not resolve to an issue or pull request" for both, so no wrong-repo #22/#23 exists to collide with; in mdBook a bare #N renders as inert text, not a wrong link; and the README disambiguates in-document via full-URL links at first mention and in the Issue disposition section. The remedy (linkify or rewrite as prose) is proportionate hygiene, but as stated it is incomplete — applied alone it would leave the same pattern in PLAN.md's plan/0072 row — and arguably premature, since the queued enforcement plan will sweep the corpus mechanically when cut.
survivors.corrected_claim: After correctly linking the first mentions to full GitHub URLs, the README uses bare "#22"/"#23" fifteen times, including two verify lines ("#23 closed", "#22 closed"). This adds new instances to the operator's recorded-but-not-yet-enforced follow-up (PLAN.md follow-up table: "Enforce full GitHub URLs... Future plan, not yet cut"), matching the link-first-then-bare pattern the corpus and PLAN.md's own plan/0072 index row already use, rather than breaching an in-force doctrine. Ambiguity is contained: agentic-host has no issues #22/#23, and both references are disambiguated by full-URL links elsewhere in the same document; the cost is future cleanup when the enforcement plan lands, plus dead (non-navigable) references in the published site.
survivors.severity_adjust: advisory, not minor — the doctrine's own record defers enforcement ("Future plan, not yet cut"), the pattern matches current committed practice including PLAN.md's plan/0072 index row, no concrete misdirection is possible (no agentic-host#22/#23 exists), and none of the review's defect classes (rework, wrong verdict, hollow green, unbuildable task, broken dependency) follows from executing the plan as written.

survivors.claim: checklist-reporter "renders the full registry over a series" — including mail- and forge-lane mechanical results and mail-lane's attested items — but depends only on series-lane and build-receipts, so under the stated-dependencies-override rule parallel dispatch can build the golden-output test and cut its receipt before those lanes exist, and the output (and receipt) go stale when they land; further, the plan never says how path-scoped results (maintainer-cc, oversize, DMARC, PR-title) reach the one output the consumer reads for the submission go/no-go — the subcommand takes no format-patch directory or PR metadata — nor that they render unrun there, so the checklist may not actually serve the dual-path submission decision it exists for.
survivors.evidence: "### checklist-reporter: `checklist` renders the full registry over a series: mechanical results from the lanes, expensive results from receipts, the widened attested set ... — depends: series-lane, build-receipts"; sequencing rule: "Tasks with no stated dependency follow the previous task; stated dependencies override."; mail-lane and forge-lane each state "depends: msg-lane" only and are otherwise consumed only by docs-and-release's "everything above"; no task defines how their results compose into the checklist or golden output.
survivors.remedy: Add mail-lane and forge-lane to checklist-reporter's depends; specify path-scoped composition (accept an optional mail directory / PR reference, or render those rules unrun with a pointer to the lane command) and cover both in the golden-output test — or scope the golden output to the lanes present and record that the docs-and-release gate re-verifies the full rendering.
survivors.verify_evidence: Verified against the README, issue #22's opening post, consolidated revision, public-signals addendum, and the digest. (1) Dependency gap is mechanically real: README's sequencing rule "Tasks with no stated dependency follow the previous task; stated dependencies override" plus checklist-reporter's "depends: series-lane, build-receipts" (identical in the consolidated revision) means mail-lane and forge-lane ("depends: msg-lane" each, otherwise consumed only by docs-and-release's "everything above") are not ancestors of checklist-reporter; a dependency-honoring parallel executor can cut its golden-output test and receipt before either lane exists. (2) Composition gap is confirmed by the design record's own interface: the opening post defines "`host-lint ffmpeg checklist <base>..<head>`: the full registry over a series, mechanical results filled in" — a commit range only — while mail checks take "`host-lint ffmpeg mail <dir>`" and forge checks need PR metadata; the consolidated revision ("As designed, plus the widened attested set") and the public-signals addendum (checklist delta adds only attested items) never amend the inputs even as the mail lane gains path-scoped mechanical rules (maintainer-cc, 1000 kB oversize, DMARC, thread-targeting, patchwork post-send) and forge-lane is created. No text in any layer says how those results reach the checklist or that they render unrun there; the "unrun renders unrun, never passed" language and the grep verify ("no unrun leg renders as passed") are scoped to expensive legs, a build-receipts term. Two calibrations, neither verdict-flipping: "the one output the consumer reads for the submission go/no-go" slightly overstates (the opening post says the checklist output is "clean enough to paste into submission notes", but host-adoption's acceptance also records separate `series`/lane runs and `mail <dir>` gates by exit code); and the staleness horn is bounded — docs-and-release depends on "everything above" and the tool-carried release runs the verify gate, so a stale golden output forces rework but cannot ship silently. Both bounds are consistent with the stated minor severity, and the remedy's render-unrun-with-pointer alternative is the honest fix the tier discipline implies.

survivors.claim: The code.ffmpeg.org account plus API token and the DMARC-safe sending address depend on no pack code and are long-lead human provisioning actions (forge signup, choosing or standing up a sending domain whose _dmarc record is not reject/quarantine), yet the plan first surfaces them inside the follow-up recorded at re-pin-and-receipt — after all nineteen build tasks — so the submission campaign waits on provisioning that could have run in parallel from day one.
survivors.evidence: re-pin-and-receipt: "a code.ffmpeg.org account plus API access for the forge path, a DMARC-safe sending address for the mailing-list path" appear only in the recorded follow-up; neither appears in "Open decisions for the operator" nor anywhere earlier, despite the plan already carrying an operator-facing decisions section.
survivors.remedy: List both as operator provisioning items now, beside the five open decisions, so they are complete before host-adoption cuts.
survivors.verify_evidence: README re-pin-and-receipt (plan/0072-ffmpeg-commit-rule-pack/README.md lines 441-443) is the sole place the provisioning items appear: "a code.ffmpeg.org account plus API access for the forge path, a DMARC-safe sending address for the mailing-list path" — verified absent from "Open decisions for the operator" (lines 136-152) and everywhere earlier; mail-lane's "DMARC pre-send warn" (line 336) is a pack check, not the provisioning item. No-code-dependency confirmed: forge-lane verifies via "the anonymous API" (line 350) and the digest's lb=False correction reads "Anonymous read access to code.ffmpeg.org/api/v1 works today (HTTP 200 ... with no token)". However, the "long-lead" premise fails against the digest: the 2025-07-22 announcement says accounts are created by "login or sign up for an account on code.ffmpeg.org" via "OpenID or Github" (minutes, not long-lead), and the DMARC item is one DNS query on the existing sending address, with lead time only if that domain publishes reject/quarantine. The "submission campaign waits" consequence is also overstated: host-adoption is "cut there once the release exists" and its acceptance runs need the released pack, so early provisioning saves only the provisioning tail beyond release day — near zero in the common case. Mitigating context: the placement is inherited, not a transplant error — #22's public-signals addendum itself allocates both items to "host-adoption (pgs-release): the project config gains the maintainer table above, a forge account plus API access for the Forgejo path, and a DMARC-safe sending address for the ML path". The remedy (list both beside the five open decisions) is cheap and proportionate; it stands.
survivors.corrected_claim: The code.ffmpeg.org account plus API access and the DMARC-safe sending address depend on no pack code (forge checks run over the anonymous API) and surface only in re-pin-and-receipt's follow-up record, never in the operator-facing "Open decisions" section — mirroring #22's addendum, which allocates them to the out-of-scope pgs-release host-adoption milestone. Surfacing them beside the five open decisions is a cheap heads-up worth adding, but they are not long-lead in the common case (forge signup is an OpenID/GitHub sign-up; the DMARC item is one DNS check, with lead time only if the operator's domain publishes reject/quarantine), and the submission campaign gates on the pack release regardless, so the schedule exposure is a small provisioning tail, not day-one parallelism lost.
survivors.severity_adjust: advisory, not minor — the claimed schedule cost rests on the unsupported "long-lead" premise; what remains is a judgment-call improvement to the operator-facing surface of a plan that already records both items in the place the design record put them.

survivors.claim: The plan phrases maintainer-cc as "must appear in the Cc list" while its sole source wording is a stated preference, and the digest explicitly recommends warn-not-fail tier; with mail-lane rules structurally outside the calibration corpus (see the tier-freeze blocker), an inline "must" is exactly where an unmeasured flag tier creeps in — and today's mandatory-CC set for the consuming project is empty, so any flag tier would be all downside.
survivors.evidence: Plan mail-lane: "maintainer-cc (a CC-marked MAINTAINERS entry matching a touched path must appear in the Cc list...)"; digest ROUND2[2]: "the rule's live wording is a stated preference ('prefers to be CC-ed') ... tier maintainer-cc as community-enforced/attested (warn on missing Cc, not hard fail)" and "the mandatory-CC set for pgs-release paths is currently empty".
survivors.remedy: State the warn tier inline (as the plan does for its neighbors: "cover-letter warn", "DMARC pre-send warn") or add maintainer-cc to the registry's uncalibratable-tier annotations.
survivors.verify_evidence: README mail-lane (plan/0072-ffmpeg-commit-rule-pack/README.md line 334) reads "maintainer-cc (a CC-marked MAINTAINERS entry matching a touched path must appear in the Cc list...)" with no tier label, while the same sentence inline-labels every neighbor: "cover-letter warn", "per-message 1000 kB flag", "70-character prose-wrap advisory", "uncompressed-attachment advisory", "DMARC pre-send warn". The digest quotes are verbatim-accurate (signals-digest.md lines 333-337): "The rule's live wording is a stated preference ('prefers to be CC-ed')", "tier maintainer-cc as community-enforced/attested (warn on missing Cc, not hard fail)", and "the mandatory-CC set for pgs-release paths is currently empty". The finding in fact understates its case: the issue #22 public-signals addendum — the record the plan claims to transplant — already settled the tier explicitly: "New mail-lane check maintainer-cc: ... Warn tier: the wording is a stated preference and developer.html contains no CC instruction, so MAINTAINERS is the sole source." So the README dropped a tier the design record had assigned, not merely a digest recommendation. The obvious refutation ("the issues are the record, the README needn't restate tiers") fails because the README does restate tiers for the five neighboring mail-lane rules — the selective omission plus "must" is the mislead vector. The calibration backstop also cannot catch this: corpus-calibration runs over "several thousand recent accepted upstream commits", and mail-lane depends only on msg-lane (not corpus-calibration); Cc headers of mailed series are absent from accepted git history, so a wrongly-flag-tiered maintainer-cc would never be measured or demoted, exactly as the finding argues. One rhetorical overreach that does not change the verdict: with today's empty mandatory-CC set the check fires never, so a flag tier would be inert now rather than "all downside" — the harm materializes only when a CC marker later appears on a pgs-touched path. Remedy (inline "warn" label, matching the paragraph's own convention, or a registry uncalibratable-tier annotation) is correct and proportionate. Advisory severity stands: the authoritative issue record carries the correct tier, so the defect is a contained mislead vector in the restated summary rather than a settled-wrong tier.

survivors.claim: checklist-reporter's verify line asserts a universal invariant ("no attested item ever renders as checked and no unrun leg renders as passed") with a grep over a golden-output fixture, which proves only that one rendering — the "ever" claim needs a renderer-level property test (proptest is already in the plan's toolbox) or a type-level guarantee, otherwise the verify line itself over-claims its tier.
survivors.evidence: checklist-reporter verify: "golden-output test; a grep asserts no attested item ever renders as checked and no unrun leg renders as passed".
survivors.remedy: Add a proptest over arbitrary registry/verdict states asserting the rendering invariant, keeping the golden-output test for format stability.
survivors.verify_evidence: Read /mnt/c/Users/dconnolly/Development/agentic-host/plan/0072-ffmpeg-commit-rule-pack/README.md in full, host-lint issues #22 and #23 with comments via gh, and the relevant sections of signals-digest.md. The quote is verbatim: the checklist-reporter task's receipt reads "verify by: golden-output test; a grep asserts no attested item ever renders as checked and no unrun leg renders as passed" (README lines 374-375), transplanted unchanged from the consolidated revision on #22, so the over-claim originates in the design record. The defect is real as stated: a grep over a golden-output fixture verifies one concrete rendering, while "ever" quantifies over all registry/verdict states; and the plan makes verify lines load-bearing ("the verify lines here are the receipts' checks", line 168) under its own no-hollow-green discipline (lines 64-67), so a receipt check wording a universal it cannot establish is exactly the tier over-claim the pack polices in the FFmpeg corpus. Checked the strongest refutation — that spec-obligations already handles it: that task (depends on checklist-reporter) mandates "ffmpeg-pack.allium modelling the verdict lifecycle and rule tiers", exercises=-linked obligations under --strict-discharge, and "proptest over open input spaces" (lines 413-421), a plausible home for a property-level discharge, but the plan never names the rendering invariant as an obligation or proptest target (Kani is scoped to "byte-level predicates", the one named proptest instance is the msg-lane grammar), and a verdict-lifecycle model need not extend to the reporter's rendering map where the guarded bug would live — so the mitigation is uncommitted and the finding survives. Severity advisory is correct: the golden test catches the common regression and the semantic half ("a leg that did not run is reported unrun, never passed") is separately design text in build-receipts with its own fixture, so the defect is contained to the verify line's honesty about its own tier. The remedy (proptest over arbitrary registry/verdict states, golden test retained for format stability) is proportionate and uses machinery the plan already mandates; an equally plan-consistent alternative is stating the rendering invariant as an explicit ffmpeg-pack.allium obligation with an exercises=-linked proptest discharged in spec-obligations. The signals digest contains nothing bearing on the reporter's rendering verification.

(area-prefix-blocker-fails-measured-ground-truth, vague-variants-unfalsifiable)") and its verify line ends with the dedicated check: "proptest over the grammar; exact-string tests for the vague list." The README's msg-lane verify (plan/0072-ffmpeg-commit-rule-pack/README.md lines 254-256) reads "verify by: the measured ground-truth subjects from #23 join the fixtures as a must-pass corpus; synthetic violations fire each rule exactly once; proptest over the grammar" — the exact-string clause is dropped, and "exact-string" appears nowhere else in any README verify line (only in the task text at line 247). survivors.claim: The README's parenthetical "oversize mail stalls silently in an unwatched moderation queue" overstates the verified source, which says the queue is checked irregularly, is manually approvable, and has a documented escalation path — nuance the pack's remediation text needs.
survivors.evidence: README mail-lane: "the per-message 1000 kB flag (oversize mail stalls silently in an unwatched moderation queue)". Digest (FAQ verbatim, verified live): oversize messages are "automatically held in the moderation queue and must be manually approved by a mailing list admin"; "The queue is not checked on a regular basis. You can ask on the #ffmpeg-devel IRC channel on Libera Chat for someone to approve your message."
survivors.remedy: Reword to "a moderation queue not checked on a regular basis" and carry the #ffmpeg-devel IRC escalation path into the rule's remediation text as the digest specifies.
survivors.verify_evidence: README mail-lane (plan/0072 README, line 331-332): "the per-message 1000 kB flag (oversize mail stalls silently in an unwatched moderation queue)". Checked against the digest and the design record: (1) "stalls silently" is not the README's invention — it is the digest's own load-bearing characterization: the digest's tier-change impact line reads "the failure mode is a silently stalled submission", and its verified summary is even stronger than the README's: "An oversize (moderated) message is effectively lost without manual escalation". Issue #22's public-signals addendum likewise says "flag tier is justified by the silent-stall failure mode". So half the allegedly overstated parenthetical is a faithful transplant. (2) "unwatched" is a real one-word compression drift: the verified FAQ text is "The queue is not checked on a regular basis. You can ask on the #ffmpeg-devel IRC channel..." — irregularly checked with a documented escalation path, not never-watched. (3) The finding's consequence clause ("nuance the pack's remediation text needs") is mis-anchored: the remediation text is authored from the design record, not from this parenthetical, and the record already carries the nuance verbatim — #22's addendum: "held in a moderation queue that 'is not checked on a regular basis' (escalation: ask on #ffmpeg-devel IRC)", and the digest impact line already mandates "the remediation text should mention the #ffmpeg-devel IRC escalation path". The README explicitly disclaims that role: "This README does not restate the rule tables or the finding bodies; the issues are the record" (README lines 16-17). The remedy's second half ("carry the IRC escalation path into the rule's remediation text as the digest specifies") therefore asks for something the plan's authoritative sources already specify; only the one-word reword survives.
survivors.corrected_claim: The README parenthetical's word "unwatched" mildly overstates the verified FAQ text ("not checked on a regular basis", with a documented #ffmpeg-devel IRC escalation path); "stalls silently" is the digest's and #22-addendum's own characterization and stands. No remediation-text nuance is at risk: the design record the mail-lane rule is built from already carries the exact quote and the IRC escalation path, and the README explicitly does not restate rule bodies. At most, reword "unwatched" to "irregularly checked" in the parenthetical.
survivors.severity_adjust: Advisory is already the floor and matches what survives; the surviving defect is a one-word wording-fidelity nit in a plan-level parenthetical with no downstream effect on the rule's remediation text.

refuted.claim: rule-registry's verify line "the freshness check fails on a pin older than the newest developer.texi commit" cannot run offline/deterministically as written — knowing the newest upstream developer.texi commit requires a live upstream query — and the plan does not say whether the CI test is fixture-based or live; run live it contradicts the offline-gates stance, run fixture-only the live staleness question silently belongs to upstream-drift-lane, which the plan never states.
refuted.why: Read plan/0072 README in full, the #22 public-signals addendum (via gh), the digest's freshness finding, and plan/0042's verify-field doctrine. All three legs of the finding fail. (1) The verify line is a negative behavioral test — "the freshness check FAILS on a pin older than the newest developer.texi commit" — a property relative to a given source history, dischargeable deterministically against a fixture history (the registry machinery is already tree-parameterized: "rules --verify-source <tree>"), and it sits beside an explicitly doctored-input clause ("a doctored section raises drift while an edit elsewhere does not"), so fixture style is the natural in-context reading; nothing in it requires a live upstream query. (2) No offline contradiction: the plan places the live half of the check "at encode time" twice — settled architecture line 88 "the registry pins upstream and carries a corpus-freshness check at encode time" and the rule-registry task lines 230-231 — matching the design record ("add a corpus-freshness check comparing the pin date against the latest doc/developer.texi commit at encode time", digest ROUND2/addendum delta). Encode time is the operator's encoding pass, which necessarily has upstream in hand (the same pass pins "a genuine upstream master SHA"); it is not an offline CI gate. (3) Post-encode staleness ownership is stated, not silent: lines 74-75 "a separate network-having lane fails when ffmpeg.org drifts from what the corpus acknowledges", and the upstream-drift-lane task watches developer.html, compares "each rule-bearing section against the digest the corpus acknowledges", fails on unacknowledged drift, with "the offline gates never depend on this lane". Remedy check: clauses (a) and (c) of the proposed remedy restate existing plan text verbatim in substance; only (b) "the CI regression test is fixture-based" adds anything, an advisory-level phrasing addition already implied by the surrounding fixture-style verify clauses — not the claimed minor methodology defect.
